title: The Scotch & the Open Games
subtitle: 1.e4 e5 beyond the Italian — the Scotch, the Scotch Gambit, the Four Knights, and the Petrov

concepts.name: Breaking with d4
concepts.short: In the Scotch White plays an early d4 to trade off Black's e5-pawn and open the centre for the pieces.

concepts.name: The d4-knight
concepts.short: After 3...exd4 4.Nxd4 White's knight sits on a strong central square, eyeing c6, f5 and b5.

concepts.name: Gambit for development
concepts.short: The Scotch Gambit gives back the d4-pawn to grab fast development and aim at f7.

concepts.name: Symmetrical development
concepts.short: The Four Knights develops all four knights first — a solid, classical way to fight for the centre.

concepts.name: The fork trick
concepts.short: ...Nxe4 followed by ...d5 regains the piece by forking two minor pieces — Black's standard equaliser.

concepts.name: Counter-attacking the e-pawn
concepts.short: The Petrov ignores defending e5 and hits White's e4 instead, steering toward a symmetrical, solid game.

concepts.name: ...d6 before ...Nxe4
concepts.short: In the Petrov Black must play 3...d6 to dislodge the knight BEFORE recapturing on e4 — grabbing at once walks into a trap.

lessons.title: Beyond the Italian
lessons.summary: Why these openings exist and what they all share.

intro.text: You already know the Italian: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4. Today we go beyond it. After 2.Nf3 Nc6, instead of the quiet bishop move White can strike the centre at once with 3.d4 — the Scotch. We will also meet the Four Knights and Black's solid reply, the Petrov. The thread tying them together: open lines and rapid development.

coach.text: This is the crossroads. White has played 2.Nf3 and Black defended with 2...Nc6. The Italian goes 3.Bc4. The Scotch goes 3.d4, challenging e5 directly. The Four Knights goes 3.Nc3. Each leads to an OPEN game — pieces out fast, the king's safety decided early.

Black has another idea entirely: 2...Nf6, the Petrov. Black does not defend e5 — Black counter-attacks e4. Same spirit, open and principled, but Black equalises by copying. We will learn how to handle it without falling for its famous trap.

intro.text: Open positions reward sharp eyes. Find the tactic in each — the kind that decides games once the centre is open.

lessons.title: The Scotch
lessons.summary: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.d4 — open the centre and post the knight.

segments.title: The main line, move by move

intro.text: Walk through the Scotch's backbone. Listen to WHY each move is played, not just what.

coach.text: 1.e4 — claim the centre, free the bishop and queen.
1...e5 — Black meets you head-on: an Open Game.
2.Nf3 — develop with a threat. The e5-pawn is now attacked.
2...Nc6 — the natural defence of e5.
3.d4 — the Scotch! Instead of 3.Bc4 you strike the centre. e5 is hit a second time and cannot hold.
3...exd4 — Black must take; defending with 3...d6 concedes the centre and 3...exd4 is correct.
4.Nxd4 — recapture with the knight. Look where it stands: a dominant central post hitting c6, b5 and f5. This is the heart of the Scotch.
4...Nf6 — Black develops and attacks e4 in return. 4...Bc5 is the other main move, hitting the d4-knight.
5.Nc3 — defend e4 and develop. White has easy, harmonious development and a space edge — the Scotch's lasting promise.

intro.text: The sharpest main line runs 4...Nf6 5.Nxc6 bxc6 6.e5 — pushing the pawn to gain space and chase the knight.

coach.text: White has played 5.Nxc6 bxc6 6.e5. The pawn on e5 cramps Black and attacks the f6-knight. Black's reply is forced-ish: the knight must find a good square while hitting back. The move is 6...Qe7, pinning the e5-pawn to White's king.

After 6...Qe7 7.Qe2 the queens face off down the e-file with the e5-pawn between them. White keeps the extra space; Black will play ...Nd5 and ...g6 to untangle. The verdict: a small, durable White pull — exactly what the Scotch is for. You do not need to memorise deeper; understand the structure.

intro.text: Your turn. Make the move that defines this opening.

coach.text: Black just played 2...Nc6, defending e5. This is the Scotch — strike the centre. What is the move?

successLine.text: 3.d4! e5 is attacked twice and the file will open. The Scotch is on the board.

retryLine.text: Not yet. The whole point of the Scotch is the immediate central break — push the d-pawn two squares.

coach.text: Black took: 3...exd4. Recapture on d4 with the piece that belongs there — the one that gains the great central post.

successLine.text: 4.Nxd4! The knight dominates the centre, eyeing f5 and b5. Note: 4.Qxd4 also recaptures but ...Nc6 hits the queen with tempo — the knight recapture is cleaner.

retryLine.text: Recapture toward the centre. The knight on f3 is the natural piece — it lands on a powerful square.

lessons.title: The Scotch Gambit
lessons.summary: 4.Bc4 — give back the pawn for development and pressure on f7.

intro.text: Instead of 4.Nxd4 you can play 4.Bc4 — the Scotch Gambit. You leave the d4-pawn alone and develop with threats.

coach.text: 4.Bc4! The bishop slices toward f7 — Black's most vulnerable square — and White will castle and play c3 to recover d4 with interest. You are temporarily a pawn down, but ahead in development. That trade is the gambit's whole idea.

A common line: 4...Nf6 5.e5, gaining space and kicking the knight. The pawn on e5 and the Bc4 both stab at the kingside. Black must play accurately (5...d5!) to survive — and at the board, the side with the initiative usually wins the practical fight.

intro.text: Black has just played 3...exd4. Enter the Scotch Gambit.

coach.text: Develop the bishop to its most aggressive diagonal, aiming straight at f7 — and offer the d4-pawn for fast development.

successLine.text: 4.Bc4! The Scotch Gambit. You will castle, play c3, and hammer f7. A pawn is a small price for a raging initiative.

retryLine.text: This is a gambit — don't rush to win d4 back. Develop a piece with a threat: the bishop belongs on the f7 diagonal.

lessons.title: The Four Knights
lessons.summary: 3.Nc3 Nf6 — develop all the knights, then fight for the centre.

intro.text: The Four Knights is the most classical Open Game: get every minor piece out before committing.

coach.text: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 — the standard Open Game start.
Black mirrors.
2.Nf3 — attack e5.
2...Nc6 — defend it.
3.Nc3 — develop the second knight and reinforce e4. This is the Four Knights move-order.
3...Nf6 — Black completes the symmetry, attacking e4. Now all four knights are out.
4.Bb5 — the Spanish Four Knights, the soundest try, pinning the c6-knight and pressuring e5 indirectly. (4.Bc4 is playable but allows a famous trick — next.)
4...Bb4 — Black copies, pinning your c3-knight. Symmetry holds; the game is balanced and rich. White breaks it later with d3, Bxc6 and a timely Nd5.

intro.text: If White plays the natural 4.Bc4, Black has a clever equaliser you must know — from BOTH sides.

coach.text: White has played 4.Bc4. It looks fine — but e4 is only defended by the c3-knight. Black strikes: 4...Nxe4! Surely that just loses a knight to 5.Nxe4? Watch the point.

After 5.Nxe4 Black plays 5...d5! — one pawn forking the bishop on c4 and the knight on e4 at the same time. Black wins one of them back and regains the piece with a comfortable game. THIS is why 4.Bb5 is preferred: it does not hang e4 to the trick.

intro.text: You are Black. White just played the careless 4.Bc4. Punish it.

coach.text: e4 is guarded only by the knight on c3. Take it — the recapture runs into a fork.

successLine.text: 4...Nxe4! Now 5.Nxe4 d5 forks the bishop and knight, and Black is fine. If instead 5.Bxf7+ Kxf7 6.Nxe4, material is already even — Black has the bishop pair and White only shuffled the king; 6...d5 simply kicks the knight back and Black is comfortable.

retryLine.text: The combination starts with a capture on e4. Grab the pawn — the follow-up ...d5 will fork your way out.

coach.text: White recaptured: 5.Nxe4. Now play the one pawn move that hits both the bishop and the knight.

successLine.text: 5...d5! A textbook fork-trick: the pawn attacks Bc4 and Ne4 together. Black regains the piece and equalises. File this idea away — it appears all over the Open Games.

retryLine.text: One pawn can attack two pieces. Which central pawn push hits both c4 and e4 at once?

lessons.title: The Petrov / Russian Defence
lessons.summary: 2...Nf6 — counter-attack e4 instead of defending e5, and avoid the famous trap.

segments.title: Copy, but in the right order

intro.text: The Petrov is Black's rock-solid weapon. The move order is everything — learn it precisely.

coach.text: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 — White attacks e5.
Black answers in the centre.
2.Nf3 — the threat on e5.
2...Nf6 — the Petrov! Black ignores the threat to e5 and counter-attacks e4. Tit for tat.
3.Nxe5 — White grabs the pawn. Now the critical question: how does Black recapture?
3...d6! NOT 3...Nxe4. First kick the e5-knight away. Recapturing on e4 at once walks into a trap we will see in a moment.
4.Nf3 — the knight retreats. NOW e4 is safe to take.
4...Nxe4 — with the white knight off e5, Black recaptures the pawn safely. Material is level.
5.d4 — White grabs the centre and opens lines for the pieces.
5...d5 — Black supports the e4-knight and stakes a central claim. The position is symmetrical and very solid: the Petrov's calling card.
6.Bd3 — develop and challenge the e4-knight. White has a tiny pull from the move; Black is rock-solid. A typical Petrov tabiya.

intro.text: Here is why move order matters. Grabbing e4 immediately loses the queen to a discovered check.

coach.text: Black played the greedy 3...Nxe4? White answered 4.Qe2! — pinning ideas and threatening the e-file. Black must NOT develop naively. The only good move is 4...Qe7, when 5.Qxe4 d6 wins the knight back. But what happens if Black plays a 'natural' developing move like 4...Nf6?

After 4...Nf6?? White plays 5.Nc6+!! The knight moves with DISCOVERED CHECK — the Qe2 now checks the king down the open e-file. Black must deal with the check, and the c6-knight forks the queen on d8. White wins the queen for a knight. THIS is why Black plays 3...d6 first.

intro.text: You are Black in the Petrov. White just took on e5 with 3.Nxe5. Show you know the move order.

coach.text: Do NOT grab e4 yet — that runs into Qe2 and a discovered-check trap. First evict the knight on e5 with a pawn.

successLine.text: 3...d6! Correct order. The knight must move, and only THEN do you take on e4 safely. Discipline over greed.

retryLine.text: Not yet — taking e4 now allows Qe2 and the Nc6+ trap. Play the pawn move that attacks the knight on e5 first.

coach.text: Different game: Black already played the risky 3...Nxe4 and White replied 4.Qe2. There is exactly ONE move that doesn't lose. Block the e-file and oppose the queen.

successLine.text: 4...Qe7! The only move. Now 5.Qxe4 d6 hits the e5-knight and Black wins it back, level material. Any developing move instead loses the queen to 5.Nc6+.

retryLine.text: A developing move walks into Nc6+ winning the queen. You must answer the queen on the e-file directly — meet it with your own queen.

lessons.title: Plans & Practice
lessons.summary: The recurring plans of the Open Games, then a tactical cool-down.

intro.text: Forget memorising twenty moves. Carry these ideas into every Open Game.

coach.text: Scotch plan (White): the centralised d4-knight and the e4-pawn give you a space edge. Castle, develop the bishop (often to e2 or e3), and look for f5 or b5 outposts for the knight. Trade when ahead in space, keep it simple.

Petrov plan (Black): you are symmetrical and solid. Finish development (Bd6, O-O, Nc6 or c6), keep the e4-knight strong, and aim for a clean, equal middlegame. The Petrov's strength is that it gives the opponent nothing to attack.

Gambit plan (White): when you sacrifice a pawn (here Scotch Gambit, 4...Bc5), you must play FAST. Castle, play c3 to hit d4 and open lines, and target f7. Initiative is the currency you bought with the pawn — spend it before Black consolidates.

intro.text: Open positions punish carelessness. Find the winning shot in each.

questions.prompt: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6, which move defines the Scotch Game?
questions.explain: 3.d4 is the Scotch — an immediate central break. 3.Bc4 is the Italian, 3.Nc3 the Four Knights, 3.Bb5 the Ruy Lopez.

questions.prompt: What is the point of the Scotch Gambit (4.Bc4 instead of 4.Nxd4)?
options: It wins a pawn by force
It trades queens to reach an endgame
It gives back the d4-pawn for fast development and pressure on f7
questions.explain: A gambit trades material for time: White leaves d4, develops with threats, castles and targets f7.

questions.prompt: In the Petrov after 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Nxe5, why should Black play 3...d6 instead of grabbing with 3...Nxe4?
options: Because 3...Nxe4 4.Qe2 sets a trap that can win Black's queen (5.Nc6+)
questions.explain: 3...Nxe4 4.Qe2 Nf6?? 5.Nc6+ is a discovered check that nets the queen. 3...d6 first evicts the knight, then ...Nxe4 is safe.

questions.prompt: In the Four Knights, why is 4.Bb5 preferred over 4.Bc4?
options: 4.Bc4 allows the fork trick 4...Nxe4 5.Nxe4 d5 regaining the piece
questions.explain: After 4.Bc4 e4 is only guarded by the c3-knight, so 4...Nxe4! 5.Nxe4 d5 forks Bc4 and Ne4 and Black equalises. 4.Bb5 avoids this.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6. You want to challenge e5 a second time and open the centre at once. Play it.
questions.explain: 3.d4 — the Scotch break, attacking e5 a second time and opening the centre.

questions.prompt: 3.d4 exd4. Recapture so your recapturing piece lands on a strong, uncontested square — not one Black can hit for free.
questions.explain: 4.Nxd4 — the knight lands on a powerful central square. 4.Qxd4 allows ...Nc6 hitting the queen with tempo.

questions.prompt: Same position, but you'd rather leave the d4-pawn and race ahead in development. Make a developing move that also eyes Black's weakest square.
questions.explain: 4.Bc4 — the Scotch Gambit, aiming the bishop at f7 and trading the pawn for the initiative.

questions.prompt: You are Black; White has just played 4.Bc4. Notice e4 has only one defender. Find the capture that begins a combination to win it back.
questions.explain: 4...Nxe4! After 5.Nxe4 d5 forks the bishop and knight, regaining the piece.

questions.prompt: Continuing as Black, White recaptured 5.Nxe4. One pawn push attacks two pieces at once — find it.
questions.explain: 5...d5! The pawn forks Bc4 and Ne4; Black wins one back and equalises.

questions.prompt: Petrov, you are Black: 2...Nf6 3.Nxe5. Before you regain the pawn, deal with the intruder on e5 — what do you play?
questions.explain: 3...d6! The knight must retreat; only then is ...Nxe4 safe. 3...Nxe4 first runs into the Qe2/Nc6+ trap.

questions.prompt: Petrov: 3...d6 4.Nf3. The e5-knight is gone, so the danger has passed. Now take the e4-pawn you were waiting for.
questions.explain: 4...Nxe4 — with White's knight off e5 there is no Qe2/Nc6+ trick, so Black regains the pawn and the game is level. Order over greed.

questions.prompt: You are Black: you grabbed 3...Nxe4 early and White hit you with 4.Qe2. Only one reply saves the game — meet the queen head-on.
questions.explain: 4...Qe7! Opposing the queen on the e-file. After 5.Qxe4 d6 Black regains the knight. A developing move instead loses to 5.Nc6+.

questions.prompt: Petrov trap sprung. After 3...Nxe4 4.Qe2 Nf6??, White played 5.Nc6+. Judge this move.
questions.explain: 5.Nc6+ is a discovered check — Qe2 checks down the open e-file — and the knight forks the queen on d8. White wins the queen.

questions.prompt: Four Knights. After 4.Bc4 Nxe4 5.Nxe4, Black played 5...d5. Judge Black's move.
questions.explain: 5...d5 is the fork trick: the pawn attacks both the bishop on c4 and the knight on e4, so Black regains the piece and equalises.

questions.prompt: Petrov trap. After 3...Nxe4 4.Qe2, Black 'developed' with 4...Nf6. Judge Black's move.
questions.explain: 4...Nf6?? loses the queen. With the e-file open and the queen on e2, White plays 5.Nc6+ — a discovered check that simultaneously forks the queen on d8. Black had to play 4...Qe7.
